item.title: Microsoft is ruining Windows customization — 4 reasons why
item.itunes:title: Microsoft is ruining Windows customization — 4 reasons why
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Customizing your Windows PC is still one of the most fun things you can do with it. Changing the behavior and looks of your PC can make it work better for you, and it's usually worth the effort of setting everything up.

Over the years, though, and especially since Windows 10, customizing Windows has become more and more difficult, and the effects you can get out of it have been reduced. I think back to something like Stardock's WindowBlinds and how it could change everything from the taskbar to the Start menu and even the window chrome for most apps. These days, you don't get fun stuff like that anymore, and Windows 11 is more boring than ever.

4 More locked-down UI elements

Customization is getting harder

Part of the reason Windows customization is getting more difficult is that Microsoft keeps adding new elements to Windows that add complexity and make it harder to devvelop customization tools. A tool like WindowBlinds used to work fairly well, but there were only a handful of elements that needed to be themed: the taskbar, Start menu, and window chrome.

However, Microsoft has since added things like the notification center, the quick actions panel, or the Widgets pane, all following the same design language, and all being more locked-down than before, especially because of how content is presented in some of them. Widgets feature a lot of web content that changes a lot, so theming is probably a lot more complicated.

These UI elements are also more streamlined behind the scenes, so it's harder to inject UI changes into these parts of the UI. You can tell it's become difficult because Stardock sort of replaced WindowBlinds with an app called Curtains for Windows 10, but then for Windows 11, WindowBlinds was brought back in a less capable form, while Curtains is nowhere to be seen. It's just a mess.

 Related
WindowBlinds review: Make Windows 11 (almost) look like anything you want

Stardock's coolest customization app is held back by changes to Windows 11 and some frustrating apps.

3 Constant updates

Windows is always changing

Another big hurdle for Windows customization is that the operating system is constantly changing with updates that are more frequent and bigger than ever. Monthly updates aren't anything new, but Microsoft has been making updates bigger and bigger in nature, frequently changing UI elements or adding elements to them. A lot of updates can even be done through server configuration changes, which makes it unpredictable when certain elements might change.

Before Windows 10, Windows would remain fairly static for a few years. Windows 7 came out in 2009, and you wouldn't get any major updates that changed the UI after that. Even when Windows 8 came out, you'd have to buy it again if you wanted it, so there was a guarantee that at least some people would keep using it. Now, we get annual updates that make huge sweeping changes to the OS, and all these updates are free to download, so there's just too much that can be broken at any given time. For developers, it would be a constant race against the clock trying to update apps to make sure they work with each and every update for Windows, which just isn't worth it for most of them. The costs involved would be too high.

 Related
8 best Windhawk mods to make your Windows 11 PC more interesting

Improve your Windows 11 experience

4

2 Streamlining the code

When old customization methods are removed

On top of all of this, Microsoft has sometimes actively gone out of its way to make it harder for certain customization apps to work. Last year, with Windows 11 version 24H2, the company removed the Windows 10 taskbar implementation from Windows 11 completely. A lot of apps used this implementation to enable features like moving the taskbar to a different edge of the screen, and suddenly, it was gone.

That's not to say I think old code should be kept around necessarily, but when the new implementation is more limiting, removing these features causes problems. A lot of developers of Windows customization tools, such as StartAllBack, had to scramble to build their own from-the-ground-up implementation of the taskbar to be able to retain the features they used to offer. Thankfully, the more well-known apps were able to step up and address it, but it makes things that much more difficult.

Plus, Microsoft even blocked the Windows 11 24H2 update on PCs that had these apps for a while, which made it more likely people would uninstall them to get the latest version of Windows.

 Related
StartAllBack review: Bringing back classic Windows goodness

Don't like the Windows 11 design changes? StartAllBack takes you back to better times, with some other improvements to boot.

1 Marking customization apps as malware

ExplorerPatcher has suffered

Finally, Microsoft has even gone so far as to block the installation of some customization programs by labeling them as malware. This is thankfully not the case for most apps, but ExplorerPatcher — a relatively popular free app — has warnings on its GitHub page about being labeled as malware.

Granted, this is likely because the app tries to add files in system folders, but this kind of measure makes users more wary of installing customization software on their PC, which hurts the space as a whole. It would be nice if Microsoft opened up more ways for apps and programs to customize Windows so that there isn't as much of a risk associated with customization apps like this.

 Related
I finally set up ExplorerPatcher on Windows, and I should have sooner

Windows 11 is much better this way

4

Windows customization isn't what it used to be

I don't know if I'm alone in feeling this way, but it feels as though Windows has become a lot more boring as time has gone on. And frankly, it's not that Windows is even alone in that. If you look at phones, Android has also become much more streamlined than it used to be.

But while I think making things more straightforward can make them more approachable, removing the option to do more out-there customizations is also something of a downside. I miss being able to make my Windows PC look like it's in its emo phase. Even if I wouldn't actually do it now, I think this has stripped some of the joy out of using a PC, especially as a kid who's curious and interested in messing with things. WindowBlinds is a core memory from my younger teenage years, and it's unfortunate that you can't get that kind of experience easily these days.

 Related
I use these 4 apps to completely customize my Windows 11 PC

Customize Windows 11 the way you want

item.content:encoded:
Customizing your Windows PC is still one of the most fun things you can do with it. Changing the behavior and looks of your PC can make it work better for you, and it's usually worth the effort of setting everything up.

Over the years, though, and especially since Windows 10, customizing Windows has become more and more difficult, and the effects you can get out of it have been reduced. I think back to something like Stardock's WindowBlinds and how it could change everything from the taskbar to the Start menu and even the window chrome for most apps. These days, you don't get fun stuff like that anymore, and Windows 11 is more boring than ever.

4 More locked-down UI elements

Customization is getting harder

Part of the reason Windows customization is getting more difficult is that Microsoft keeps adding new elements to Windows that add complexity and make it harder to devvelop customization tools. A tool like WindowBlinds used to work fairly well, but there were only a handful of elements that needed to be themed: the taskbar, Start menu, and window chrome.

However, Microsoft has since added things like the notification center, the quick actions panel, or the Widgets pane, all following the same design language, and all being more locked-down than before, especially because of how content is presented in some of them. Widgets feature a lot of web content that changes a lot, so theming is probably a lot more complicated.

These UI elements are also more streamlined behind the scenes, so it's harder to inject UI changes into these parts of the UI. You can tell it's become difficult because Stardock sort of replaced WindowBlinds with an app called Curtains for Windows 10, but then for Windows 11, WindowBlinds was brought back in a less capable form, while Curtains is nowhere to be seen. It's just a mess.

 Related
WindowBlinds review: Make Windows 11 (almost) look like anything you want

Stardock's coolest customization app is held back by changes to Windows 11 and some frustrating apps.

3 Constant updates

Windows is always changing

Another big hurdle for Windows customization is that the operating system is constantly changing with updates that are more frequent and bigger than ever. Monthly updates aren't anything new, but Microsoft has been making updates bigger and bigger in nature, frequently changing UI elements or adding elements to them. A lot of updates can even be done through server configuration changes, which makes it unpredictable when certain elements might change.

Before Windows 10, Windows would remain fairly static for a few years. Windows 7 came out in 2009, and you wouldn't get any major updates that changed the UI after that. Even when Windows 8 came out, you'd have to buy it again if you wanted it, so there was a guarantee that at least some people would keep using it. Now, we get annual updates that make huge sweeping changes to the OS, and all these updates are free to download, so there's just too much that can be broken at any given time. For developers, it would be a constant race against the clock trying to update apps to make sure they work with each and every update for Windows, which just isn't worth it for most of them. The costs involved would be too high.

 Related
8 best Windhawk mods to make your Windows 11 PC more interesting

Improve your Windows 11 experience

4

2 Streamlining the code

When old customization methods are removed

On top of all of this, Microsoft has sometimes actively gone out of its way to make it harder for certain customization apps to work. Last year, with Windows 11 version 24H2, the company removed the Windows 10 taskbar implementation from Windows 11 completely. A lot of apps used this implementation to enable features like moving the taskbar to a different edge of the screen, and suddenly, it was gone.

That's not to say I think old code should be kept around necessarily, but when the new implementation is more limiting, removing these features causes problems. A lot of developers of Windows customization tools, such as StartAllBack, had to scramble to build their own from-the-ground-up implementation of the taskbar to be able to retain the features they used to offer. Thankfully, the more well-known apps were able to step up and address it, but it makes things that much more difficult.

Plus, Microsoft even blocked the Windows 11 24H2 update on PCs that had these apps for a while, which made it more likely people would uninstall them to get the latest version of Windows.

 Related
StartAllBack review: Bringing back classic Windows goodness

Don't like the Windows 11 design changes? StartAllBack takes you back to better times, with some other improvements to boot.

1 Marking customization apps as malware

ExplorerPatcher has suffered

Finally, Microsoft has even gone so far as to block the installation of some customization programs by labeling them as malware. This is thankfully not the case for most apps, but ExplorerPatcher — a relatively popular free app — has warnings on its GitHub page about being labeled as malware.

Granted, this is likely because the app tries to add files in system folders, but this kind of measure makes users more wary of installing customization software on their PC, which hurts the space as a whole. It would be nice if Microsoft opened up more ways for apps and programs to customize Windows so that there isn't as much of a risk associated with customization apps like this.

 Related
I finally set up ExplorerPatcher on Windows, and I should have sooner

Windows 11 is much better this way

4

Windows customization isn't what it used to be

I don't know if I'm alone in feeling this way, but it feels as though Windows has become a lot more boring as time has gone on. And frankly, it's not that Windows is even alone in that. If you look at phones, Android has also become much more streamlined than it used to be.

But while I think making things more straightforward can make them more approachable, removing the option to do more out-there customizations is also something of a downside. I miss being able to make my Windows PC look like it's in its emo phase. Even if I wouldn't actually do it now, I think this has stripped some of the joy out of using a PC, especially as a kid who's curious and interested in messing with things. WindowBlinds is a core memory from my younger teenage years, and it's unfortunate that you can't get that kind of experience easily these days.

 Related
I use these 4 apps to completely customize my Windows 11 PC

Customize Windows 11 the way you want

item.title: 3 reasons I'll never vertically mount my graphics card
item.itunes:title: 3 reasons I'll never vertically mount my graphics card
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Vertical GPU mounting is, without a doubt, a sleek way to showcase your high-end graphics card, especially when you've splurged on a premium triple-fan model with RGB lighting and metallic accents. Considering how much I love turning my PC into a visually appealing showcase, you'd think I'd go all-in with vertical GPU mounting, which has become popular among PC enthusiasts chasing aesthetics. However, that's far from the case.

I'd argue that visual appeal shouldn't come at the cost of practicality and performance, which is why I'd never mount my graphics card vertically in my PC. I've experimented long enough to realize that vertical mounting is just not worth the significant trade-offs that outweigh the aesthetic charm. So, if you're on the fence about buying a PCIe riser cable and wondering whether vertical mounting would be worth all the extra effort, keep reading, because if you don't have realistic expectations, you will be disappointed.

 Related
5 obvious PC building mistakes that you're still guilty of

Overshadowed by bigger blunders, these PC building mistakes can fly under the radar

12

3 GPU temperatures get worse

Your graphics card struggles to get enough air

This is my biggest issue with vertical mounting; sure, it makes your GPU the star attraction in your case, but it comes at the cost of higher operating temperatures. When you vertically mount your GPU in a standard PC case, the fans sit very close to the tempered glass side panel. This proximity significantly restricts airflow, reducing the cooler's ability to intake fresh air efficiently. As a result, you'll immediately notice an increase in your GPU temperature, which is typically around 5-10 degrees Celsius.

Considering how high-end graphics cards—even the ones with beefy cooling solutions—already run hot in standard configuration, a 10 °C increase can potentially cause thermal throttling issues. Remember that modern GPUs need to run cooler to be able to boost their clock speeds higher, so if you're looking to get the most performance out of your expensive GPU, you may want to steer clear of vertical mounting. Additionally, this configuration disrupts airflow patterns carefully designed by case manufacturers; you're basically compromising thermal efficiency by chasing aesthetics.

2 You trade sagging for riser cable issues

It's a solution to an aesthetic problem with compromises

With modern graphics cards getting thicker and heavier, sagging has become a concern among many PC enthusiasts. Some prefer to mount them vertically instead of just getting a GPU sag bracket. Although GPU sagging isn't a problem when you mount your graphics card vertically, many users overlook other critical issues that plague this configuration. For instance, the PCIe riser cables you need to get to mount your GPU vertically are notorious for a few reasons. When using cheap riser cables, your graphics card may fail to POST. They may also cause system instability and introduce visual artifacts while gaming.

Cheaping out on PCIe riser cables is not a good idea, but even some expensive, high-quality riser cables aren't immune to these problems, especially in the long run. At some point down the line, you could very well encounter a BSOD, and you'll struggle to figure out exactly what the problem is. And don't forget that not every case comes with a native vertical mounting option, so you may have to resort to aftermarket brackets or DIY mods that often lead to poor fitment and increased stress on the PCIe slot.

1 PCIe performance degradation

PCIe riser cables are prone to signal loss

You probably already know that PCIe operates at extremely high data transfer rates, especially the modern PCIe 4.0 and 5.0 standards. However, since the PCIe riser cables extend the distance (between the motherboard's PCIe slot and the GPU) and complicate the signal path, cheap quality ones can cause signal degradation, potentially lowering the bandwidth available for your graphics card. Even if your motherboard supports the latest PCIe standard, it may downgrade speeds from PCIe 5.0 to PCIe 4.0 or from x16 to x8 when it detects signal instability.

Riser cables introduce another physical layer where interference can creep in and introduce instabilities, especially if the cable is long, bent at sharp angles, or routed near the PSU. In worst-case scenarios, you may experience unexpected crashes or performance degradation that's hard to pinpoint. What's the point of spending over $1,000 on a high-end graphics card if your PCIe riser card throttles its performance? As a gamer, frame rates matter way more to me than mere aesthetics.

Vertical GPU mounting rarely justifies the cost and hassle

High-quality riser cables cost anywhere between $50 and $100, which is a substantial amount to pay for looks alone. And if your PC doesn't natively support vertical mounting, the total cost will be even higher. It feels more like a vanity purchase than a functional upgrade. And let's not forget the potential issues you may have to deal with down the line. My top priority is making sure my components run cool, and the system remains stable under intense workloads. With a riser card, I'd have to sacrifice my peace of mind for aesthetics. I know for a fact that I'd be constantly monitoring my GPU temperatures using MSI Afterburner and get worried every time it exceeds 80C. That's precisely why horizontal mounting will always remain my preferred way of installing a GPU. Sure, GPU sag can be a problem in the long run, but sag brackets are pretty cheap.

 Related
4 reasons Asus' GPU sag detection is unironically a big deal

It's not just another marketing gimmick

3
